coredata.dc:title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots based core-satellite hybrid spheres and their use as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of mercury(II) ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Mengjuan
Wang, Chengquan
Qian, Jing
Wang, Kan
Yang, Zhenting
Liu, Qian
Mao, Hanping
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: We herein proposed a simple and effective strategy for preparing graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based core-satellite hybrid spheres and further explored the feasibility of using such spheres as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for the visual determination of Hg2+. The red-emitting CdTe QDs were firstly entrapped in the silica nanosphere to reduce their toxicity and improve their photo and chemical stabilities, thus providing a built-in correction for environmental effects, while the GQDs possessing good biocompatibility and low toxicity were electrostatic self-assembly on the silica surface acting as reaction sites. Upon exposure to the increasing contents of Hg2+, the blue fluorescence of GQDs can be gradually quenched presumably due to facilitating nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation. With the embedded CdTe QDs as the internal standard, the variations of the tested solution display continuous fluorescence color changes from blue to red, which can be easily observed by the naked eye without any sophisticated instrumentations and specially equipped laboratories. This sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ in a broad linear range of 10 nM–22 μM with a low detection limit of 3.3 nM (S/N = 3), much lower than the allowable Hg2+ contents in drinking water set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. This prototype ratiometric probe is of good simplicity, low toxicity, excellent stabilities, and thus potentially attractive for Hg2+ quantification related biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Core-satellite hybrid spheres
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visual determination
Mercury(II) ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Encapsulation of CdTe QDs in silica nanospheres
Preparation of the dual-emission CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres
Procedures of the spectrometric and visual detection of Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
Characterization of the core-satellite CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres
Spectrometric and naked eye determination of Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the GQDs on ultra-thin carbon support film. (B) AFM image of the GQDs on mica substrates with the height profiles along the line in image. (C) FTIR spectrum of the resulting GQDs. (D) Absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of the as-prepared GQDs. Inset: photographs of the aqueous GQDs under day light (a) and 365 nm UV light (b). (E) The excitation-independent emission spectra of the GQDs. The excitation wavelength of GQDs from a to h: 300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, and 440 nm.

(A) UV–vis spectra of CdTe QDs (a) and CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres (b). Inset: TEM image of CdTe QDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs (λ ex = 365 nm) of CdTe QDs (a) and CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres (b). TEM images of the as-prepared CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres (C) and the CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres (D).

Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding fluorescence colors (λ ex = 365 nm) of the as-obtained CdTe@SiO2@GQDs ratiometric probes (A), ratiometric probe-Hg2+ (50 μM) mixture (B), and ratiometric probe-Hg2+ (50 μM)-cysteine (1200 μM) mixture (C). (D) The structure of the ratiometric fluorescence probe and its working principle for Hg2+ determination. (E) The selectivity of the ratiometric probe to different metal ions (50 μM) in the 10 mM PBS (pH 8.0).

(A) Curves of the relative fluorescence intensity of CdTe@SiO2@GQDs versus irradiation time under UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm). (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of the GQDs (a) and CdTe QDs (b) in the CdTe@SiO2@GQDs ratiometric probes at different pH values. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CdTe@SiO2@GQDs in 10 mM PBS when pH was shifted between 4.0 and 10.0. (D) Relationship between (I′0-I′)/I′0 and pH values. I′0 =(I465/I650)0 and I'=(I465/I650) were the ratio of the ﬂuorescence intensity of the ratiometric probe in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (22 μM), respectively.

Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding ﬂuorescence colors of the ratiometric probes (A) and single GQDs (B) upon the exposure to different concentrations of Hg2+ (from a to k: 0, 10, 300, 1500, 4000, 6300, 10000, 15000, 22000, 32000, and 50000 nM). (C) Relationship between I'/I′0 and Hg2+ concentrations. Inset: the calibration curve of detection (n = 5). (D) Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding ﬂuorescence colors under a UV lamp (inset) of the ratiometric probes in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations (from a to c: 30, 500, and 2200 nM) in Campus Lake water. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Determination results of Hg2+ in Campus Lake water (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots based core-satellite hybrid spheres and their use as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of mercury(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We herein proposed a simple and effective strategy for preparing graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based core-satellite hybrid spheres and further explored the feasibility of using such spheres as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for the visual determination of Hg2+. The red-emitting CdTe QDs were firstly entrapped in the silica nanosphere to reduce their toxicity and improve their photo and chemical stabilities, thus providing a built-in correction for environmental effects, while the GQDs possessing good biocompatibility and low toxicity were electrostatic self-assembly on the silica surface acting as reaction sites. Upon exposure to the increasing contents of Hg2+, the blue fluorescence of GQDs can be gradually quenched presumably due to facilitating nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation. With the embedded CdTe QDs as the internal standard, the variations of the tested solution display continuous fluorescence color changes from blue to red, which can be easily observed by the naked eye without any sophisticated instrumentations and specially equipped laboratories. This sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ in a broad linear range of 10 nM–22 μM with a low detection limit of 3.3 nM (S/N = 3), much lower than the allowable Hg2+ contents in drinking water set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. This prototype ratiometric probe is of good simplicity, low toxicity, excellent stabilities, and thus potentially attractive for Hg2+ quantification related biological systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which compare fluorescence intensities of two different wavelengths before and following analyte recognition, have attracted increasing attention currently [1–3]. Compared to those single emission sensors, the signal variation of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors combining the intensity ratio of the dual wavelengths, is independent of the probe concentrations, environmental effects, as well as the fluctuation of excitation light intensity, thus efficiently improving the accuracy and sensitivity of the quantification [4–6]. With one emission as the reference, a slight decrease of the other emission intensity can produce distinguishable fluorescence color changes from the original background, affording the convenience of visual detection of analytes by the naked eye under a UV lamp [7–9]. Till now, the ratiometric fluorescence probes in most of the works are made of fluorescent organic dyes, which are always susceptible to photobleaching, low quantum yield, and narrow excitation profile and broad emission bands [10,11]. Such notorious properties combined with the challenge to design suitable organic molecules and their complicated synthesis and purification processes often make them difficult to construct efficient ratiometric fluorescence probes [12].

Compared with the organic dyes, traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) exhibit unique optical properties such as broad excitation spectra and size-tunable narrow emission, high fluorescence quantum efficiency, high extinction coefficients, and strong resistance to photobleaching [7,13]. These excellent optical properties and especially the size-dependent emission of traditional QDs make them a superior choice for the fabrication of ratiometric fluorescence probes by incorporating two kinds of QDs or two differently sized QDs into one core-satellite hybrid nanosphere. For example, the blue emitting CdS QDs were firstly embedded in chitosan (CS) nanospheres to obtain CdS@CS; and the CdS@CS@CdTe was finally synthesized by the electrostatic attraction of CdTe QDs on CdS@CS surface [14]. These CdS@CS@CdTe nanospheres have been empolyed as the visual fluorescence probe for the detection of trace Hg2+. Some other Cd chalcogenide QDs-based hybrid nanospheres have been hybridized by embedding the red-emitting CdTe QDs [9,15–17] or CdTe/CdS QDs [18] into silica nanoparticles and covalently linking the green-emitting one onto the silica surface. Following the “on-off” or “on-off-on” detection mode, these dual-emission hybrid probes with desired intensity ratios have been demonstrated to be promising for the visual detection of 5-fluorouracil [9], trinitrotoluene [15], cysteine/homocysteine [16], Cu2+ [17], and Hg2+ [18]. Although these traditional QDs have great potential for the ratiometric fluorescence probes fabrication, certain limitations may restrict their practical use because those Cd-based QDs that are highly toxic to both cell and live animals due to their inherent chemical composition, size, shape, and surface [19–23]. In particular, surface oxidation of Cd-based QDs via air-induced or UV-catalyzed oxidation led to the formation of reduced Cd on the QD surface and liberation of free Cd2+ ions, and correlated with cell death [22,23]. Encapsulating Cd-based QDs by inert materials such as silica is a recognized method to reduce their toxicity [24,25]. Besides, silica coating can also enhance the chemical and optical stability of QDs and pave way for further functionalization of the silica surface [26,27]. Although the embedded Cd-based QDs show little or no deleterious effects when their surfaces are fully passivated with appropriate modification, another kind or sized QDs coated on the surface of the silica nanospheres and thus served as the response signal and exposed to the external environment directly, can still possess the potential cytotoxicity, genotoxicity, and neurotoxicity in practical use [20].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), single or few-layer graphene with a size smaller than 100 nm [28,29], have ignited tremendous research interest for their applications in electronic and optical fields due to their unique quantum confinement and edge effects [30,31]. Unlike traditional semiconductor QDs, GQDs possess superior properties like good biocompatibility, excellent solubility, high electrical conductivity, low toxicity, stable fluorescence, and easy labeling, thus making them at the center of significant research fields like catalysis, optoelectronic devices, biosensors and bioimaging [32,33]. However, the practical implementation of GQDs in sensing applications is still in its initial stages: only a few single emission sensors in the form of signal-off and signal-off-on modes based on energy transfer [32,34], charge transfer [35,36] or other mechanisms [37–40] have been reported for protein kinase [32], monosaccharides [35], glucose [39], trinitrotoluene [34], Hg2+ [36,37], free chlorine [38], phosphate [40]. Due to the aforementioned disadvantages of the single emission-based fluorescent sensors and the potential toxicity of the exposed Cd-based QDs, design and synthesis of the GQDs-based non-toxic ratiometric fluorescence probe for onsite naked eye determination applications is of great significance.

Mercury (II) ion (Hg2+) is one of the widespread global pollutants [36]. The extreme toxicity of Hg2+ coming from its high affinity for thiol groups in proteins and enzymes finally results in the dysfunction of cells [41,42]. Its frequent and fruitful use can lead to a high level of residual and consequently result in the contamination of water systems [43]. US Environmental Protection Agency has set a limit that the Hg2+ levels in drinking water cannot exceed 10 nM [44]. Due to the high intrinsic sensitivity of fluorescence analysis, a series of fluorescent analysis have been reported for Hg2+ detection but only displaying a decrease or increase in the single sensing signal [42–44]. In this work, we demonstrate the first preparation of the GQDs-based ratiometric probe for the highly sensitive and onsite naked eye determination of Hg2+. The red-emitting CdTe QDs were firstly entrapped into the silica nanosphere to reduce their toxicity and improve their photo and chemical stabilities thus providing a built-in correction for environmental effects, while the GQDs were electrostatic self-assembly onto the silica surface acting as reaction sites for Hg2+. In the presence of increasing contents of Hg2+, the ratiometric probe exhibits continuous fluorescence color changes from blue to red, which affords the convenience of the onsite naked eye determination of Hg2+ without any sophisticated instrumentations and specially equipped laboratories.

Tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), and poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA, MW: 100,000–200,000, 20% w/w) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Tellurium powder (99%), CdCl2•2.5H2O, NH3•H2O (25%), NaBH4 (99%) and ethanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 10 mM PBS was prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. All the reagents were used without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the study.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images using a JEOL 2100 TEM (JEOL, Japan) were performed at 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) data were obtained by tapping-mode on a Bruker Dimension Icon (Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from Nicolet Nexus 470 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). Fluorescence spectra and the fluorescence intensity (FL intensity) were measured on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at room temperature. Zeta potential was got using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (UK). All the photographs were taken by a Canon digital camera (IXUS 230 HS, China) under the UV light (365 nm).

In a modified procedure of GQDs preparation by pyrolysis of citric acid [45], 2 g of citric acid was put into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C by a heating mantle for about 30 min, until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, the liquid was dissolved into 100 mL of 0.25 M NaOH solution drop by drop, with continuous vigorous stirring. The obtained GQDs solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 with 2 M HCl solution and stored in the refrigerator for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation of CdTe QDs in silica nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs were synthesized in the aqueous phase by two reaction steps according to our reported method (Supporting Information) [46]. The CdTe QDs embedded silica (CdTe@SiO2) nanoparticles were prepared by a modified Stöber method [15,18]. Typically, 30 mL of ethanol and 5 mL of red-emitting CdTe QDs solution were mixed in a 100 mL flask and stirred for 5 min at room temperature. After packing the flask with aluminum foil, 100 μL of APTS was introduced, and the resultant solution was stirred for 6 h. Then, 0.5 mL of TEOS was added dropwise into the solution, followed by adding 0.5 mL of NH3•H2O, and finally the mixture was left to react for another 24 h. After that, the products were centrifuged and the precipitate was washed with ethanol and water several times. Finally, the resulting CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres were redispersed in 9 mL of water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the dual-emission CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres
ce:section.ce:para: To modify the resulting CdTe@SiO2 with the surface of positive charge, 6 mL CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres were added into 40 mL of PDDA (1%, v/v), and the mixture was kept stirring vigorously for 2 h in the dark. After centrifugation and washing, the CdTe@SiO2-PDDA nanospheres were obtained and redispered in 14 mL of water, followed by the addition of 6 mL of the GQDs. After stirring for another 1 h and being purified, the CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres were obtained and redispered in 6 mL of water for further use. The amount of GQDs used in the synthesis of CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres was excessive, as evidenced by the observed blue fluorescence from the supernatant fluid under UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures of the spectrometric and visual detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: 30 μL of the as-prepared CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres and a certain volume of Hg2+ containing solution were added into a 5 mL tube, the mixture was diluted with 10 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 8.0) to a final volume of 200 μL and reacted for 1 min at room temperature to obtain solution A. To perform the visual fluorescence detections, solution A was transferred to a series of miniwells and the photographs were obtained using a digital camera under UV illumination (λ ex = 365 nm) in a dark box. To perform the spectrometric analysis, solution A was diluted to 2 mL with 10 mM PBS (pH = 8.0) and the fluorescence spectra of the diluted mixture solutions were recorded with the λ ex of 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real water samples were got from our Campus Lake and filtered twice using ordinary qualitative filter paper and a 0.20 μm membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. After that, water samples were adjusted to pH 8.0 by adding PBS. Different concentrations of Hg2+ were added to the as-prepared water samples, and their fluorescence photographs and spectra were subsequently recorded.

GQDs were obtained by direct pyrolysis of citric acid using a slightly modified version of the previously reported procedure [45]. The as-made GQDs are well-dispersed, well-separated from each other with an average diameter of 10 nm (Fig. 1 A), which is consistent with the previous report [47]. The AFM results (Fig. 1B) demonstrate that the height of the resulting GQDs is mostly distributed in the range of 0.5–3.0 nm with an average value of 1.9 nm. The FTIR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1C) shows peaks located at about 3420 and 1070 cm−1, which can be attributed to the characteristic absorption bands of the –OH stretching vibration [48]. The characteristic absorption band of C–OH stretching at 1295 cm−1 is also observed. There are strong bands centered at 1580 and 1400 cm−1, which can be ascribed to the anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively [48]. The appearance of these hydrophilic groups including –COOH and/or–OH makes the GQDs soluble in aqueous medium [34,45]. It is clear that stretching vibration of C–H in aromatic rings around 3000–3100 cm−1 and skeletal vibration of aromatic rings around 1450–1650 cm−1 (characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compound) are not found in the recorded FTIR spectrum, implying that the citric acid has been carbonized completely [45].

A well-defined absorption band at 352 nm is observed in the UV–vis spectra of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs (Fig. 1D), assigned to the π−π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [49]. Note that the GQDs dispersion has optimal maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 378 nm and 465 nm, respectively. The photograph of the dispersion shows a yellow color (in web version) under visible light (inset a in Fig. 1D) and gives blue fluorescence emission under the excitation by 365 nm UV light (inset b in Fig. 1D), further indicating the resulting GQDs are fluorescent. The emission wavelength of GQDs is nearly excitation-independent, with the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength at 380 and 465 nm, respectively (Fig. 1E). The excitation-independent emission and the well-defined absorption band of the GQDs, implies that both the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters contained in GQDs should be uniform, which may contribute to their strong fluorescence emission [34,45].

The diameter of the resulting MPA-capped CdTe QDs is mainly distributed in the range of 2–5 nm with an average diameter of 3.5 nm, as determined by the TEM image (Inset in Fig. 2 A). The UV–vis (curve a in Fig. 2A) and fluorescence (curve a in Fig. 2B) spectra show that the as-prepared CdTe QDs have a maximum absorption peak at 572 nm and they emit strong fluorescence with the maximal emission wavelength of 643 nm. The particle size can be calculated to be 3.44 nm in virtue of the reported expression [50], which is consistent with the results of the TEM image. CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres have been synthesized through a typical Stöber method with some modification [15,18]. Specifically, CdTe QDs were initially conjugated to an amine-containing silane agent APTS, and then APTS and a silica alkoxide precursor TEOS were allowed to hydrolyze and co-condense in the ethanol/NH3•H2O mixture, which resulted in CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres. The UV–vis absorption (curve b in Fig. 2A) and fluorescence (curve b in Fig. 2B) spectrum of CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres remains nearly unchanged in comparison with that of the mother CdTe QDs, which implies that the encapsulated CdTe QDs are well electronically isolated from each other [51]. Photographs of aqueous dispersed CdTe QDs (a) and CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres (b) under UV lamp are shown in inset of Fig. 2B. Both of them emit bright red fluorescence. The TEM image indicates that the as-prepared CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres (Fig. 2C) have a chemically clean and homogenized structure with an average diameter of 110 nm. The existence of the silica shell has several unique benefits. Firstly, the silica shell can effectively prevent the air-induced or UV-catalyzed surface oxidation and the leakage of toxic Cd2+ ions, thus cutting down the toxicity of the CdTe QDs [24,25]. Secondly, an additional silica layer improves the photo and chemical stabilities of CdTe QDs and also prevents their direct contact with the external analytes, thus providing a reliable reference signal [52]. Thirdly, the outer silica shell offers an ideal anchorage substrate for the subsequent binding of GQDs to produce a core-satellite hybrid spheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the core-satellite CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres
ce:section.ce:para: The planting of the GQDs onto the surface of the CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres was achieved through the electrostatic interaction. The surfaces of the CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres were terminated by hydroxyl groups and thus were negatively charged with a zeta potential of −22.52 mV at neutral pH. The adsorption of cationic polyelectrolyte PDDA on CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres switched the zeta potential to a positive value of 34.9 mV. The zeta potential of GQDs was measured to be −15.9 mV and thus possessing negatively charged surfaces were subsequently attached to the surface of the CdTe@SiO2 via electrostatic self-assembly. The zeta potential of CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres was −6.2 mV, which showed that GQDs had acted with CdTe@SiO2, and the surface of CdTe@SiO2@GQDs was negatively charged. Coating of GQDs on CdTe@SiO2 was also demontrated by the TEM image (Fig. 2D) and the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3 A). After the coating process, many dark dots were clearly observed on the surface of the CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres, which is the evidence of the successful formation of the CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres (Fig. 2D). The resulting CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres emit well-resolved dual fluorescence emission peaks centered at 465 and 650 nm under a single wavelength excitation of 380 nm (Fig. 3A), ascribed to the fluorescence emission of GQDs and CdTe QDs, respectively. This result demonstrates that the GQDs have been successfully coupled onto the CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres' surface. The corresponding fluorescence photograph of the CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres is displayed as inset of Fig. 3A for a direct comparison with that of the GQDs (inset b in Fig. 1D) and CdTe@SiO2 (inset b in Fig. 2B). Differing from both of them, the as-prepared CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres exhibited a less intense blue color. These results further confirm that the GQDs have been successfully attached on the CdTe@SiO2 surface and all of them are photoluminescent using a single excitation. Although no GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe has been reported, other members in carbon family such as fluorescent graphene oxide (FGO) [53] and carbon dots [7] have demonstrated their efficiency for the ratiometric fluorescence probe design, through the direct electrostatic attraction of FGO on CdTe QDs [53] or by the simply mixing the carbon dots with the red-emission CdSe@ZnS QDs [7]. Without the protection of the inert materials such as silica, the Cd2+ release and the potential Förster resonance energy transfer between two kinds of lumophores cannot be effectively avoided.

The response of the hybrid spheres to Hg2+ has been monitored to prove the working principle for onsite naked eye determination of Hg2+. Hg2+ is considered to be particularly effective to quench the fluorescence of GQDs obtained by the acitric acid pyrolysis reaction [37]. As expected, the fluorescence emission at 465 nm contributed by the coated GQDs on silica surface was quenched completely upon the addition of 50 μM Hg2+, whereas the emission at 650 nm contributed by the encapsulated CdTe QDs still remained unchanged (Fig. 3B). The changes in the intensity ratio of the dual emission resulted in a remarkable fluorescence color change (photographs inset in Figs. 3A and B), thereby realizing the naked eye determination of Hg2+ onsite. Upon the “on-off” state of the blue fluorescence of GQDs, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor is therefore constructed for Hg2+ determination (Fig. 3D).

Selectivity is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of a sensing system. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity ratios (I465/I650) of the probe incubated with different metal ions at the same concentration of 50 μM are shown in Fig. 3E. The results indicated that the blue fluorescence of GQDs was almost completely quenched by Hg2+ and slightly quenched by Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, whereas the other tested metal ions induce no obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio. The outstanding Hg2+ ion selectivity can be ascribed to the strong afﬁnity between Hg2+ and the carboxylic (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) functionalities on GQDs surface over other metal ions (Fig. 3D) [48]. The selective fluorescence quenching of GQDs by Hg2+ is presumably due to the facilitating nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation via an effective electron or energy transfer from the GQDs to Hg2+ ions [48,54]. If a strong Hg2+ chelator, cysteine, is added into the probe + Hg2+ sensing system, Hg2+ can be removed from the GQDs surface due to formation of the Hg–S bond with cysteine, which results in the recovered fluorescence of GQDs (Fig. 3C).

The photostability of the as-prepared probes against time is systematically examined under UV light in pH8.0 PBS (Fig. 4 A). After 8 consecutive irradiation at 365 nm (10 min for each time), the fluorescence intensity of GQDs at 465 nm versus that of CdTe QDs at 650 nm remains no signiﬁcant change in 80 min under an excitation at 365 nm, implying the photostability of the ratiometric probes in PBS solution. That is, these ratiometric probes can exhibit excellent photostability, making their use in the long-term and real-time assay possible.

The relative fluorescence intensities of the GQDs coated on the silica surface (curve a in Fig. 4B) are obviously pH-dependent and relatively low in strongly acidic solutions, while the CdTe QDs wrapped by the silica (curve b in Fig. 4B) are relatively stable in a broad solution pH range. As pH increased, the relative fluorescence intensity of the GQDs presents a monotonic increase with the emission wavelength remaining unchanged. Both the coated GQDs and the encapsulated CdTe QDs emitted strong fluorescence in neutral and alkaline system, and their fluorescence intensities keep stable in a pH range of 7.0–10.0. If the solution pH value is switched between 10.0 and 4.0, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs varies reversibly (Fig. 4C), in accordance with the spectra reported previously [55]. Fig. 4D shows that at a buffer pH value of 8.0, maximum quenching efficiency of the ratiometric nanoparticles is obtained. The low quenching efficiency in acidic medium is the result of protonation of the GQDs' surface binding carboxyls. With the increasing pH, the deprotonation of the carboxylic groups in the GQDs occurs. This may strengthen the covalent bond between Hg2+ and ratiometric nanoparticles, which leads to the decreasing fluorescence intensity ratio, namely higher quenching efficiency. However, the fluorescence intensity ratio rises with the further increase of pH value. At high pH values, Hg2+ may be complexed by OH− and convert to the corresponding Hg(OH)2 or HgO, which, in turn, reduces its complex with ratiometric nanospheres [36,41]. Considering the fact, the buffer with a pH value of 8.0 was used for subsequent detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrometric and naked eye determination of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The dose response of the ratiometric probes to Hg2+ has been examined. Upon the addition of Hg2+ with increasing concentrations, the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm of the blue GQDs is continuously quenched, whereas the intensity at 650 nm of the embedded red CdTe QDs still remains unchanged (Fig. 5 A). Based on the internal standard of the red emission of the CdTe QDs embedded in silica, a slight decrease of the blue emission of GQDs led to an obviously distinguishable color evolution from blue to red in the Hg2+ concentration range of 300 nM–22 μΜ (bottom of Fig. 5A). A simple and low-cost visual assay for Hg2+ detection can be thus recognized by the naked eye without the help of any sophisticated instrumentations and specially equipped laboratories. Meanwhile, the advantages of the ratiometric fluorescence for visual detection of Hg2+ are testified by the comparison with the single fluorescence quenching experiment, in which only pure GQDs probe is employed for the detection of Hg2+. Unlike the ratiometric probe using dual emissions, the color changes of the single emission fluorescence quenching of the GQDs upon the addition of Hg2+ are hard to be observed (bottom of Fig. 5B), though the intensity of the blue emission decreased at the comparable level (Fig. 5B). The comparison clearly demonstrates that the ratiometric fluorescence probe possesses higher sensitivity and reliability than a single fluorescence quenching probe for the onsite naked eye determination of Hg2+.

By analyzing the ratio of the fluorescence intensity with the concentrations of Hg2+ (Fig. 5C), we obtained a linear relationship (inset in Fig. 5C) between the logarithm of the (I′/I′0) value and the concentration of Hg2+ over a range of 10 nM–22 μM, where I′0 =(I465/I650)0 and I′=(I465/I650) are the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric probe in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Hg2+, respectively. The linear curve fits a regression equation of log(I'/I′0) = −5.00 × 10− 5 c Hg2+ – 0.025 with R 2 = 0.9965 and a detection limit of 3.3 nM based on S/N = 3, which is much lower than other previously reported values and lower than the maximum level (10 nM) for Hg2+ in drinking water permitted by the United States Environmental Protection Agency [44]. Characteristics of this sensor such as the detection strategy, the linear range, and the detection limit reported in the literatures have been summarized in Table S1. As can be seen, the present ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ possessed a broader linear range and a lower detection limit.

To further investigate the practicality of the sensing method, a standard addition method has been utilized to detect Hg2+ in water from our Campus Lake. After analyzing the samples, it was observed that Hg2+ ion was not detected, so Hg2+ concentrations were spiked at 300, 1500, and 22000 nM, and their corresponding fluorescence spectra (Fig. 5D) and the fluorescence colors under a UV lamp (inset in Fig. 5D) were given. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained by doing the experiment for 5 times under the same condition. According to Table 1 , the estimated recoveries of the measurements and the RSD are acceptable and satisfactory. It can be concluded that the measurement of Hg2+ ion for real water samples is feasible.

In summary, we demonstrate a simple and effective strategy for preparing CdTe@SiO2@GQDs hybrid spheres and further explored the feasibility of using such spheres as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the onsite naked eye determination of Hg2+. This sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ in a broad linear range of 10 nM–22 μM with a detection limit of 3.3 nM (S/N = 3), much lower than the allowable Hg2+ contents in drinking water set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Compared with the single fluorescence quenching of the pure GQDs probe, one can determine Hg2+ in a broad concentration range of 300 nM–22 μM by the naked eye with high resolution and accuracy. Furthermore, the proposed method can be applied to the determination of Hg2+ in real samples in Campus Lake water with satisfactory recoveries, confirming the validation of such probe in the Hg2+ sensing related biological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175061, 21375050, and 21405063), the Natural Science Foundation Of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20130481), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (2015T80517), Qinglan Project, Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology (No. NZ201109 and NZ201307), and A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No.PAPD-2014-37).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.07.042.
